role_summary: SNCC field organizer; in this corpus's embedding her testimony clusters with Charles McLaurin, Walter Tillow, Martha Prescod Norman Noonan, and Ekwueme Thelwell.
ai_reading: Todd's top semantic neighbors are Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 308), Walter Tillow (entry 124, count 281), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83, count 192), Ekwueme Michael Thelwell (entry 34, count 162), and Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons (entry 53, count 150). The cluster crosses SNCC's Mississippi voter-registration network (McLaurin), its Washington office (Thelwell), its Northern support fundraisers (Noonan, Robinson via her own cluster), and the medical/labor-organizing Tillow node. The high counts (peak 308) suggest a long, topically dense oral history. Her Tactical Pragmatism vs Strategic Vision position is +0.72, far toward Strategic Vision, joining the cluster of SNCC women whose testimony the embedding reads as strategic-framed (Avery +0.69, Henderson +0.51, Robinson -0.21 the only exception in this band).
biographical_paragraph: Lisa Anderson Todd's oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 79 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1]. Independent biographical sourcing is pending; the strongest signal currently available for movement role is the embedding pattern visible in the AI's reading above.
movement_context: The 1964 Mississippi Summer Project (Freedom Summer) was the joint Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) campaign that brought roughly 700 to 1,000 northern volunteers, the majority of them white college students, into Mississippi for a coordinated voter-registration, Freedom School, and community-center effort organized by SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC under Bob Moses's direction [src: 2, 3]. Volunteers were vetted, interviewed, and trained in two week-long orientation sessions at the Western College for Women in Oxford, Ohio, in June 1964; SNCC veterans Moses, James Forman, and others led the sessions on Mississippi conditions, non-violent discipline, and the legal apparatus the volunteers would face [src: 2, 3]. Lisa Anderson, then a Northern student volunteer, attended the Oxford orientation and traveled into Mississippi with the first wave [src: 4]. The same Oxford orientation sent off CORE worker Michael Schwerner (already in Mississippi), summer volunteer Andrew Goodman, and CORE worker James Chaney; the three left Oxford for Meridian on June 20, 1964 and were abducted and murdered by Klansmen and Neshoba County deputies on June 21, with their bodies found August 4 buried in an earthen dam outside Philadelphia, Mississippi [src: 2, 3]. The murders broke into national news as the rest of the volunteer cohort was just arriving in their assigned counties; the project continued under that shadow. The summer's political endpoint was the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) challenge at the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, where Fannie Lou Hamer, Aaron Henry, Ed King, and the MFDP delegation argued before the Credentials Committee that the all-white regular Mississippi delegation had been chosen through a registration process that systematically excluded Black voters and that the MFDP, open to all races, was the legitimate Mississippi Democratic Party [src: 3, 5]. The compromise offered (two at-large MFDP seats and a 1968 rule change requiring integrated delegations) was rejected; Hamer's televised Credentials Committee testimony, interrupted live by a hastily-arranged White House press conference President Johnson called to displace it, made the challenge a national event. Todd's role through that summer is documented in her own 2014 memoir For a Voice and the Vote: My Journey with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, which traces the Oxford orientation, her Mississippi assignment, and her participation in the Atlantic City delegation [src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: Todd's 2014 memoir For a Voice and the Vote: My Journey with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (University Press of Kentucky, hardcover ISBN 9780813146515, paperback 2016) is one of the comparatively small number of full-length first-person accounts written by a Freedom Summer volunteer that center on the MFDP convention challenge rather than on the field organizing alone [src: 4]. The book sits alongside Bruce Watson's Freedom Summer: The Savage Season That Made Mississippi Burn and Made America a Democracy (Viking, 2010), which draws on volunteer letters and interviews to reconstruct the Oxford orientation and the summer's events, and Doug McAdam's earlier sociological study Freedom Summer (Oxford University Press, 1988), which followed the volunteers' post-1964 trajectories into later movement work [src: 6, 7]. Todd has remained active in the SNCC alumni network, including the SNCC Legacy Project's documentation and public-education work in partnership with Duke University's SNCC Digital Gateway; her oral history for the Civil Rights History Project (Library of Congress, AFC 2010/039) was recorded as part of the same generational documentation effort [src: 1, 4].

gallery.alt: SNCC recruitment broadside, '1000 students wanted for the SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project,' April 1964
gallery.caption: SNCC recruitment broadside circulated in April 1964 to enlist northern college-student volunteers for the Mississippi Summer Project. Volunteers who answered the call attended the Western College for Women orientation in Oxford, Ohio in June 1964 and then traveled into Mississippi for the voter-registration, Freedom School, and community-center work that became known as Freedom Summer. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
gallery.photographer: Mississippi Department of Archives and History (broadside; SNCC)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Mississippi Department of Archives and History on Flickr Commons)

gallery.alt: Black and white Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party supporters demonstrating outside the 1964 Democratic National Convention, Atlantic City, August 24, 1964
gallery.caption: Black and white supporters of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party demonstrating outside the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey, August 24, 1964, during the MFDP credentials challenge in which Fannie Lou Hamer, Aaron Henry, Ed King, and the MFDP delegation argued that the all-white regular Mississippi delegation had been seated through a registration process that excluded Black voters. Photographed by Warren K. Leffler for U.S. News & World Report.
gallery.photographer: Warren K. Leffler (U.S. News & World Report)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress, U.S. News & World Report collection)
gallery.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions)

gallery.alt: Ohio Historical Marker 9-9, Freedom Summer 1964, near the former Western College for Women in Oxford, Ohio
gallery.caption: Ohio Historical Marker 9-9, Freedom Summer 1964, near the former Western College for Women campus in Oxford, Ohio. The Western College hosted the two week-long June 1964 orientation sessions that prepared SNCC, CORE, NAACP, and SCLC volunteers (including Lisa Anderson Todd) for the Mississippi Summer Project. The marker memorializes the orientation and the murders of CORE workers Michael Schwerner, James Chaney, and summer volunteer Andrew Goodman, who left the Oxford orientation for Meridian, Mississippi on June 20, 1964 and were killed by Klansmen and Neshoba County deputies on June 21.

sources.title: Lisa Anderson Todd oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Freedom Summer
sources.publisher: SNCC Digital Gateway, Duke University (in collaboration with the SNCC Legacy Project; working from the SNCC papers at the Robert W. Woodruff Library, Atlanta University Center)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne, I've Got the Light of Freedom: The Organizing Tradition and the Mississippi Freedom Struggle
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 1995 (second edition 2007); WorldCat OCLC 61664854 (the standard institutional history of SNCC's Mississippi organizing tradition, with chapters on Freedom Summer and the MFDP convention challenge)

sources.title: Lisa Anderson Todd, For a Voice and the Vote: My Journey with the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party
sources.publisher: University Press of Kentucky, 2014 (paperback 2016); ISBN 9780813146515; WorldCat OCLC 881387247 (Todd's first-person memoir of the Oxford orientation, her Mississippi assignment, and her participation in the MFDP Atlantic City delegation)

sources.title: Kay Mills, This Little Light of Mine: The Life of Fannie Lou Hamer
sources.publisher: Plume / Penguin, 1994 (second edition University Press of Kentucky, 2007); WorldCat OCLC 29521716 (the standard biography of Hamer, with substantial coverage of the MFDP Atlantic City challenge)

sources.title: Bruce Watson, Freedom Summer: The Savage Season That Made Mississippi Burn and Made America a Democracy
sources.publisher: Viking, 2010; WorldCat OCLC 489637746 (full-length narrative history of the 1964 project drawing on volunteer letters, the Oxford orientation, and the Neshoba County murders)

sources.title: Doug McAdam, Freedom Summer
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1988; WorldCat OCLC 17299604 (sociological study tracking the volunteer cohort through Oxford orientation, the Mississippi summer, and post-1964 movement trajectories)

sources.title: Maegan Parker Brooks, A Voice That Could Stir an Army: Fannie Lou Hamer and the Rhetoric of the Black Freedom Movement
sources.publisher: University Press of Mississippi, 2014; WorldCat OCLC 856849317 (rhetorical biography of Hamer with detailed coverage of the August 1964 Credentials Committee testimony and the MFDP delegation)
